Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it finds and returns only the most relevant documentation context so clients can get high-quality LLM answers without sending huge corpora. Key points:

- ~90% context reduction — compresses large docs (e.g., 500K tokens) down to ~5K highly relevant tokens  
- Delivers optimized context (not LLM responses) so clients use their own LLMs  
- No vendor lock-in / no pass-through LLM API costs for Snipara  
- High margins — pure compute/engineering product rather than LLM inference billing  
- LLM-agnostic — works with any MCP-compatible client (Claude, GPT, Gemini, etc.)

Snipara’s core value proposition is context optimization: deliver highly relevant, token-efficient documentation context to a client’s own LLM so the LLM can produce good answers within token limits. Key points:

- ~90% context reduction — compress large docs (e.g., 500K tokens) to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content  
- Client uses their own LLM — no vendor lock‑in and no LLM inference costs passed through  
- High margins — context optimization is pure compute (low cost) relative to inference  
- LLM‑agnostic — works with any MCP‑compatible client

Business model tie‑in: Snipara charges for the context optimization queries, not for LLM inference.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it finds, ranks and compresses the most relevant parts of a customer’s documentation so their own LLM can answer effectively within token limits. Key points:

- ~90% context reduction (e.g., from ~500K tokens to ~5K tokens of highly relevant content)  
- Customers use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in, no passthrough inference costs)  
- High margins (service is pure compute for context, not LLM inference)  
- LLM‑agnostic — works with any MCP‑compatible client  

Core insight: the real product is the optimized context (not the LLM’s response).

Short summary — Snipara uses three repositories that together form its deployment and distribution architecture:

1) monorepo: alopez3006/snipara-webapp (source of truth, production deploy)
- Contents: Next.js web app (apps/web), Python MCP server (apps/mcp-server), shared Prisma schema/client (packages/database), shared TS types.
- Role: primary development repo where features are implemented and tested. apps/mcp-server is the code that runs api.snipara.com in production (Railway service snipara-fastapi).
- Production deploy: Docker image built from apps/mcp-server in this monorepo → Railway service `snipara-fastapi`.
- Deploy command (manual): cd apps/mcp-server && railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"

2) alopez3006/snipara-mcp (PyPI client package)
- Contents: Standalone Python client package for the MCP protocol (snipara-mcp) intended for distribution on PyPI.
- Role: packaged client library (not the production server). Kept as a separate repo for packaging/publishing reasons; changes originate in the monorepo code that implements the same client functionality.

3) Snipara/snipara-server (enterprise / self‑hosted mirror)
- Contents: Enterprise / self-hosted copy of the MCP server and supporting scripts (local clone lives under /Users/alopez/Devs/snipara-fastapi).
- Role: mirror used for enterprise releases and customer self-hosting. Not the production deploy for api.snipara.com.
- Notes / caution: its init-db.sh runs prisma db push --accept-data-loss (dangerous) — keep schema in sync to avoid dropping columns.

How they relate and important operational rules
- The monorepo is the canonical source; enterprise repo must be synced from it after MCP changes (copy server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma).
- Prisma schema sync is critical: the Python Prisma schema in apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma MUST match the JS Prisma schema in packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma because both share the same Neon Postgres DB. If they drift, the enterprise repo’s db push can DROP columns.
- After any JS Prisma schema change: run pnpm db:push (apply JS schema to Neon) and then redeploy the Python MCP server with the synced Python schema (railway up ...).

Key risks and mitigations
- Risk: schema drift → lost DB columns/data. Mitigation: always copy new models/fields/enums to the Python schema and follow the sync steps (pnpm db:push then redeploy).
- Risk: MCP server deploy fails due to missing Settings fields or license init. Mitigation: grep for settings.* when adding fields; run post-deploy checks (curl /health, tools/list, tools/call) and check Railway logs.
- Risk: device-flow POSTs broken by 301 redirects. Mitigation: use www.snipara.com for API POSTs (never bare snipara.com).

Useful commands (ops checklist)
- Health check: curl -s https://api.snipara.com/health
- MCP tools/list test: curl -s -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara" -H "X-API-Key: <key>" -H "Content-Type: application/json" -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}'
- MCP tools/call test: similar curl with method "tools/call" and arguments {"name":"rlm_stats"}
- Prisma sync/deploy: pnpm db:push && cd apps/mcp-server && railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"

That is the three-component architecture: a single monorepo that drives production, a separate PyPI client repo, and an enterprise/self-hosted mirror that must be carefully synced.

3. Enterprise / self-hosted mirror (Snipara/snipara-server) — enterprise customers / ops

   - Holds the same MCP server code tailored for enterprise, includes init-db.sh that runs prisma db push --accept-data-loss (dangerous if schemas differ).
   - NOT the source of truth for production, but must be kept in sync for enterprise releases.

Key deployment & operational notes
- Production runs from the monorepo apps/mcp-server and is deployed via Railway service snipara-fastapi (api.snipara.com).
  - Example manual deploy:
    cd apps/mcp-server
    railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"
- Prisma schema MUST be kept in sync between:
  - packages/database/prisma/schema.prisma (JS schema)
  - apps/mcp-server/prisma/schema.prisma (Python Prisma generator)

  server.py, db.py, auth.py, rlm_engine.py, mcp_transport.py, usage.py, config.py, models.py, services/*.py, prisma/schema.prisma
- Production Dockerfile (monorepo) does NOT run prisma db push; JS-side migrations are applied via pnpm db:push. After JS schema changes, push JS schema then redeploy the MCP server with the synced Python schema.

Why this split exists
- One repo (monorepo) is the canonical production source with both frontend and the MCP server.
- The PyPI client is maintained separately for distribution convenience.
- The enterprise repo provides an ops-friendly mirror for customers running self-hosted instances; it must be manually synchronized to avoid dangerous schema/drift issues.

Operational checklist reminders
- After any Prisma JS schema change: copy models/fields/enums into the Python schema, then:
  pnpm db:push
  cd apps/mcp-server && railway up -d -s "snipara-fastapi" -e "production"

Short answer:
Snipara enforces a fixed token budget per context query and actively splits, ranks, compresses and allocates that budget across project docs and any shared/team context so the client’s LLM gets the most relevant ~tokens permitted. You control the budget (via max_tokens / per-project max) and Snipara guarantees the returned “optimized context” fits that budget.

How it works (details):

- Query-level budget
  - Every call to rlm_context_query / rlm_ask / rlm_plan accepts a max_tokens parameter (or uses the project default).
  - The platform returns only sections that fit within that token budget — it chunks, compresses and truncates as needed (Snipara returns optimized context, not an LLM response).

- Per-project defaults and limits
  - Projects have maxTokensPerQuery (default: 4000) in their settings; that is the default budget if you don’t override it.
  - rlm_ask is the quick tool (approx ~2,500 token budget, no extra config).
  - Use rlm_context_query for fuller, configurable budgets (examples: LITE ~3–5K tokens, FULL ~8–15K or higher if you set max_tokens).

- Shared/team context inclusion & allocation
  - When shared context (team collections) is included, Snipara divides the query budget between project documents and shared context. Example default: 60% project docs + 40% shared context (see shared context example).
  - Within shared context, token allocation follows category priorities: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (these percentages are applied to the shared-context portion of the budget).

- Chunking & token accounting
  - Documents are pre-chunked into DocumentChunks with precomputed tokenCount fields. Semantic search selects chunks and sums tokenCounts to enforce the budget.
  - Snipara may compress or summarize sections (DocumentSummary, rlm_store_summary) to reduce tokens while preserving relevance.

- Tools & workflows that expose budgeting
  - rlm_context_query — full-featured, budgeted semantic/hybrid search (use max_tokens).
  - rlm_ask — fast, predictable small budget (~2500 tokens).
  - rlm_plan / rlm_decompose — used in FULL workflows with larger budgets (examples show max_tokens up to 16k).
  - rlm_shared_context(max_tokens=…) — returns shared docs and a budget_allocation breakdown.

- Usage tracking & billing
  - Every query records inputTokens and outputTokens for billing/analytics.
  - RLM bills per context-optimization query (not LLM inference).

Practical guidance
- For quick lookups use rlm_ask (~2.5K). For implementation work follow LITE (3–5K) or FULL (8–15K+) budgets and call rlm_context_query with an appropriate max_tokens.
- If you need team rules included, call rlm_shared_context first (or rely on automatic inclusion) and expect ~40% of budget used for shared docs (allocated by category).
- Set project maxTokensPerQuery if you want a stricter per-project cap; otherwise pass max_tokens per-call for ad-hoc needs.

Risk / caveat
- If you exceed the project maxTokensPerQuery, the server will enforce the cap. Always check returned section token counts to confirm critical context wasn’t truncated. Usage (input/output tokens) is logged and used for billing.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a hard token budget per context query: it searches and ranks your docs, then selects, compresses, and/or summarizes sections so the returned "optimized context" fits within the requested token budget for your client LLM.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Query + budget input
   - You call a tool (typically rlm_context_query with max_tokens or rlm_ask for a quick ~2500‑token query). Projects also have a default maxTokensPerQuery (4000 by default).
2. Search & rank
   - Snipara runs semantic/keyword/hybrid search over DocumentChunks, scoring candidates by relevance.
3. Budget-aware selection
   - Each candidate chunk has a tokenCount (DocumentChunk.tokenCount). Snipara sums token counts and selects the highest‑scoring sections while respecting the max_tokens budget you provided.
4. Compression/chunking
   - If selected sections exceed the budget, Snipara compresses (summarizes) or re‑chunks content to fit. It also may return section summaries (DocumentSummary with tokenCount) to save tokens.
5. Shared context & allocation
   - When team shared context is enabled, the budget is split between project docs and shared collections. Example allocation used in the docs: 60% project docs + 40% shared context, and shared collections themselves allocate budget internally (MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%).
6. Strategy & tuning
   - You can control behavior via parameters (max_tokens, search_mode, strategy like "relevance_first"/"depth_first"/"breadth_first") when calling rlm_plan/rlm_context_query/rlm_decompose.
7. Accounting & telemetry
   - Input/output token usage is tracked per Query (inputTokens, outputTokens) for billing/analytics. QueryCache stores totalTokens for cached results and is invalidated when source docs change.

Practical examples & defaults
- LITE mode: typical token budgets ~3–5K tokens (most quick tasks).
- FULL mode: budgets for complex, multi‑chunk work ~8–15K tokens.
- rlm_ask: a quick tool ~2500 tokens, no extra config.
- rlm_context_query: explicit max_tokens (e.g., 6000) — Snipara will allocate across sources (e.g., 3600 project / 2400 shared), pick and compress top sections to return an optimized context ≤ 6000 tokens.

Where token counts come from
- DocumentChunk.tokenCount and DocumentSummary.tokenCount are used to measure cost of including each piece of context; embeddings and chunk metadata drive selection.

Why this matters
- Keeps context small and relevant so your own LLM can produce high‑quality answers within its token limits (the core value of “Context Optimization as a Service”).

If you want, I can show an example rlm_context_query call and the expected allocation/response for a given max_tokens value.

Short answer
- You set a token budget when you call an MCP query (rlm_context_query max_tokens, or use the project default maxTokensPerQuery). Snipara returns an OPTIMIZED CONTEXT whose total tokens are kept within that budget.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Budget source
   - Caller supplies max_tokens to rlm_context_query (or uses project.default maxTokensPerQuery).
   - rlm_ask is a shortcut with a fixed, predictable ~2.5K token budget.

2. Allocation
   - Snipara splits the budget between project documents and any shared/team context. (Example default: 60% project docs / 40% shared context.)
   - Shared context itself is budgeted by category: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (example allocation shown by rlm_shared_context).

3. Search → rank → fill
   - It performs semantic/keyword/hybrid search to find relevant DocumentChunks/sections.
   - Each chunk/summary has a precomputed tokenCount. Snipara scores/ranks sections by relevance and includes them in descending order until the allocated token sub‑budget is exhausted.
   - Sections may be compressed/chunked to fit remaining tokens.

4. Output
   - The response is the optimized context (not an LLM answer). It contains the selected sections and keeps total tokens within max_tokens.
   - Multi‑project queries use a total token budget and respect per_project_limit (max sections per project).

5. Why this matters
   - Keeps context small and highly relevant (90% reduction example), so client LLMs can produce high‑quality responses within their token limits.

Operational notes and bookkeeping
- LITE vs FULL workflows have suggested budgets (LITE ~3–5K, FULL ~8–15K).
- DocumentChunk and DocumentSummary records include tokenCount for accurate budgeting.
- Usage (inputTokens/outputTokens) is tracked per Query for billing/analytics.
- rlm_ask is useful when you want a quick, fixed‑budget query with no config.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team- or org-level repositories of reusable documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, guidelines) that projects automatically inherit and that Snipara includes in context queries.
- They let teams enforce mandatory rules and surface shared conventions to every developer/agent without copying docs into each project.

How they work (workflow)
1. Create a collection (Team Admin) and add SharedDocuments (each doc = one focused topic).
2. Set each document’s category and priority:
   - Categories: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE
   - Priority: 0–100 (ordering within a category)
3. Link the collection to projects in the dashboard (projects automatically inherit documents).
4. At query time Snipara includes shared context in results:
   - rlm_shared_context(...) fetches shared docs (you can request categories and a token budget).
   - rlm_context_query(...) automatically includes shared context when returning project context.
   - Token budgeting: shared context is allocated ~40% of the budget by default (project docs ~60%), and within shared context the category allocation is MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (example values used by the system).
5. You can inject shared context into a session so all subsequent queries / implementations see the team standards:
   - rlm_inject(context=..., append=False)

Key tools / example calls
- rlm_list_collections(include_public=true) — list available collections
- rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id=..., title=..., content=..., category=..., priority=...)
- rlm_shared_context(categories=["MANDATORY","BEST_PRACTICES"], max_tokens=4000)
- rlm_inject(context=..., append=False)
- rlm_context_query(query="...", max_tokens=6000) — includes shared context automatically

When to use shared collections
- Enforce security/architecture rules (MANDATORY)
- Provide team coding standards and best practices
- Supply templates (PR review, code review, story templates) for consistency
- Make onboarding and cross-project development consistent

Best practices
- Start with MANDATORY rules for non‑negotiables (security, API contracts)
- Add BEST_PRACTICES gradually
- Keep documents focused (one topic per document)
- Use priority to order important docs within a category
- Update collections regularly as standards evolve
- Use prompt templates stored in collections for consistent workflows (rlm_get_template)

Models & metadata (what’s stored)
- SharedContextCollection: name, slug, scope (GLOBAL/TEAM/USER), isPublic, usageCount
- SharedDocument: title, content, category, priority, tokenCount
- PromptTemplate: name/slug/prompt/variables/category

Access / billing notes
- Shared context collections are a Team/Enterprise feature (project linking and team-level inheritance require team plan capabilities).
- Collections are used to distribute standards across projects without duplicating content.

Result
- Teams get consistent, automatically-included standards and templates in every Snipara query, improving code quality, compliance, and developer speed while keeping token budgets efficient.

Short answer
Shared context collections are team- or org‑level bundles of reusable documentation (coding standards, best practices, templates, guidelines, etc.) that projects automatically inherit and that Snipara includes when answering context queries. They let teams enforce non‑negotiable rules and share conventions as part of every context optimization request.

How they work (concept + flow)
- Purpose: centralize things teams must follow (security, architecture) and recommended patterns (testing, style), so every rlm_context_query can include team rules automatically.
- Create & populate:
  1. Create a Shared Context Collection (team or global scope).
  2. Upload focused documents into the collection (one topic per doc) using rlm_upload_shared_document (category, priority).
  3. Link the collection to projects in the dashboard (projects then inherit the collection).
- Runtime usage:
  - Call rlm_shared_context(...) to fetch team standards up front (optional).
  - Inject shared context into a session with rlm_inject(...) if you want it persisted/forced for the session.
  - When you call rlm_context_query("<task>"), Snipara automatically includes linked shared collections in the returned context. Token budgeting routes a portion of the query budget to shared context.
- APIs & helpers: rlm_list_collections, rlm_upload_shared_document, rlm_shared_context, rlm_inject, rlm_context_query, rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template (for prompt templates).

Token budgeting and categories
- Documents are categorized and prioritized to control budget and importance:
  - Categories: MANDATORY (highest), BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE (lowest).
  - Typical token allocation in shared context requests: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%.
- When included automatically in project queries, shared context typically consumes ~40% of the context budget (project docs get ~60%).

Best practices for teams
- Start with MANDATORY rules (security, architecture).
- Keep documents focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priority values (0–100) to order docs within a category.
- Add BEST_PRACTICES gradually and update as standards evolve.
- Provide prompt templates and code‑review templates for consistency.
- Update collections regularly and link them to projects via the dashboard.

Example usage (summary)
- Admin uploads TypeScript standards:
  rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id="team_collection_id", title="TypeScript Coding Standards", category="BEST_PRACTICES", priority=90, content="...")
- Developer at session start:

  rlm_inject(context=shared_documents_text, append=False)
- Subsequent project query automatically returns context that includes those team rules:
  context = rlm_context_query(query="implement user registration API endpoint")

Who can use it
- Available on Team and Enterprise plans (collections can be global, team, or user scoped). Projects linked to a collection automatically inherit its documents.

Benefits
- Ensures consistency and compliance across projects
- Saves time: developers don’t have to re‑look up team rules
- Token‑efficient: only the most relevant parts of team docs are injected into each query

Short summary
- Snipara supports the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization (device) flow for clients that can’t open a browser. The server issues a device code + user code, the user authorizes via a browser, and the client polls for tokens.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Client requests a device code from the device endpoint (POST /oauth/device/code). The response includes:
   - deviceCode (long, used by the client to poll)
   - userCode (short human code shown to the user)
   - interval (polling interval, default: 5s)
   - verification URL (implicit in standard flow — show userCode and URL to the user)
2. Show the user the userCode and verification URL. The user visits the URL, signs in, and approves the client.
3. The client polls the token endpoint (using the deviceCode) at the server-specified interval until one of:
   - status → AUTHORIZED: server returns access + refresh tokens
   - status → EXPIRED / DENIED / USED: client stops and handles failure
4. On success the server issues OAuth tokens; clients use them for MCP requests with:
   - Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   (these tokens are stored hashed in the DB; only a tokenPrefix is kept for identification)

Relevant server-side details (DB / token handling)
- DeviceCode model fields: deviceCode, userCode, status (PENDING, AUTHORIZED, EXPIRED, USED, DENIED), interval (default 5s).
- OAuthToken model fields: accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash, tokenPrefix (first 12 chars for identification), scope, clientName, accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt.
- Tokens are stored hashed (access + refresh hashes); tokenPrefix is kept so admins/users can identify tokens.

Important operational note / gotcha
- Never POST to bare snipara.com for device/token endpoints — snipara.com 301→ www.snipara.com converts POST→GET and causes the device flow to silently fail (405). Always use the www hostname for API POST endpoints.

Header format quick reference
- Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_... (for OAuth tokens)
- Project/Team API keys remain available as X-API-Key: rlm_... for MCP access.

Short summary — how Snipara's OAuth Device Flow works
- Client requests a device code from the Snipara device-code endpoint (POST /oauth/device/code). Server returns a device_code, a short human user_code, a verification URI, expires_in, and a polling interval (default interval = 5s).
- The user visits the verification URL (shown to them) and enters/approves the user_code to authorize the device.
- Meanwhile the client polls the token-exchange endpoint (using the device_code) at the server’s token endpoint until the DeviceCode status becomes AUTHORIZED (or expires/denied). Polling must respect the returned interval.
- When authorized, the server returns access (and optionally refresh) tokens. The client uses the access token in requests: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Tokens are stored hashed in the DB (accessTokenHash / refreshTokenHash) and a tokenPrefix (first chars) is kept for identification; accessExpiresAt / refreshExpiresAt track expirations.
- Scopes like "mcp:read mcp:write" are applied to tokens and enforced by the MCP.

Important implementation details & gotchas
- DeviceCode model fields: deviceCode, userCode, status (PENDING, AUTHORIZED, EXPIRED, USED, DENIED), interval (poll interval), expiresAt.
- Default polling interval is 5 seconds; clients should back off on errors and stop after expires_in.
- Tokens are hashed in the DB. The tokenPrefix (first ~12 chars) is used for display/identification only.
- Use the Authorization header for OAuth tokens: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Common pitfall: POSTing to api.snipara.com can 301 → www.snipara.com and convert POST→GET, causing 405 errors for device-flow polling. Always use the www.snipara.com host for API POST endpoints.
- Typical scopes and uses: device-flow tokens carry MCP scopes (e.g., mcp:read / mcp:write) and can be used by clients that cannot do a browser-based OAuth redirect.

Troubleshooting tips
- If polling repeatedly fails with 405 or unexpected errors, confirm you’re POSTing to the www.snipara.com host (not bare snipara.com).
- Check the DeviceCode status in DB or via logs; ensure you honor the interval and stop after expiration.
- Verify token expiry and refresh flow by checking accessExpiresAt / refreshExpiresAt and that refresh token was returned/stored.

Short answer: Snipara implements the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization (device) flow for clients that can’t open a browser. The client asks the MCP for a device/user code, shows the user a short user_code + verification URL to complete auth on another device, and then polls the token endpoint until the user authorizes — at which point the MCP issues access (and refresh) tokens.

Full details (how it works in Snipara)

1. Client requests a device code
   - POST to the device-code endpoint (e.g., POST /oauth/device/code).
   - Server returns a DeviceCode object with: deviceCode, userCode, verification URL, status (initially PENDING), expires_at, and interval (polling interval, default 5s).

2. Prompt the user
   - The client displays the short userCode and the verification URL (or opens the URL on a second device).
   - The user signs in and authorizes the client on that verification page.

3. Client polls for tokens
   - The client repeatedly calls the token endpoint using the deviceCode.
   - Polling respects the returned interval (DeviceCode.interval, default 5 seconds).
   - The DeviceCode.status transitions: PENDING → AUTHORIZED (when user approves), or EXPIRED / DENIED / USED if not authorized or already consumed.

4. Token issuance and storage
   - When authorized, the MCP issues access and (optionally) refresh tokens.
   - Snipara stores token values as hashed values (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash); a tokenPrefix (first 12 chars) is kept for identification.
   - Token metadata saved: scope, clientName (e.g., "Claude Desktop"), accessExpiresAt, refreshExpiresAt.

5. Using tokens with MCP
   - Use the Authorization header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - Project/team API keys are still supported for other flows (X-API-Key: rlm_...).

6. Security & lifecycle
   - Tokens have expiry timestamps; refresh tokens (if present) allow renewing access tokens.
   - Status and expirations are enforced by the DeviceCode record.

7. Common gotcha (important)
   - Always use the full host with www for POST API endpoints (use https://www.snipara.com). Posting to snipara.com can 301-redirect to www, converting the POST to GET and causing silent device-flow failures (405). This is a known common failure mode — avoid bare snipara.com in device-flow POSTs.

Where to read more
- See docs/AUTHENTICATION.md and the DeviceCode / OAuthToken models in the schema for full fields and examples.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for day‑to‑day documentation lookups and to fetch the optimized context you need to answer or implement a single, bounded task (LITE mode).
- Use rlm_decompose when a task is complex/multi-step (FULL mode): break an implementation or plan into independent chunks/sub-queries so you can iterate chunk-by-chunk (then use rlm_context_query for each chunk).

When to pick which (decision checklist)
- Use rlm_context_query if the task is quick, single‑session, affects few files, or you just need the relevant context for an immediate question or small change.
- Use rlm_decompose if the task scores 2+ on the FULL-mode checklist (multiple sessions, 5+ files, architectural decisions, or needs to be understandable later) — i.e., anything that benefits from an execution plan and chunked work.

Typical workflows / examples
- “How does authentication work?” — rlm_context_query("authentication") → answer from returned context.
- “Implement OAuth 2.0 integration (spec exists)” — FULL flow:
  1. rlm_plan(query="Implement OAuth 2.0 integration")  
  2. rlm_decompose(query="OAuth 2.0 integration", max_depth=…) → returns sub-queries, dependencies, order, suggested budgets  
  3. For each chunk: context = rlm_context_query(chunk_query, max_tokens=…) → implement + test → rlm_remember/store summary
- For quick, small lookups you can also use rlm_ask (faster, predictable ~2500 token budget).

What each tool returns / responsibility
- rlm_context_query: semantic + hybrid search, ranking/scoring, token-budgeted optimized CONTEXT (sections) you pass to your LLM or runtime.
- rlm_decompose: structural decomposition of a large task into sub-queries, dependency graph, suggested execution order and budgets (it does not fetch full context for each chunk).

Practical tips & rules from the docs
- Always try Snipara MCP first — start with rlm_context_query for documentation/code lookups. If it errors (500/timeout) fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob.
- In FULL mode, follow the Phase flow: rlm_plan → rlm_decompose → use rlm_context_query per chunk → implement + tests → rlm_remember/rlm_store_summary.
- Keep token budgets in mind: rlm_context_query is for optimized context (3–6K tokens in LITE, 8–15K in FULL flows).
- Use rlm_ask for fast/simple queries and rlm_multi_query/rlm_multi_project_query when you need parallel or cross‑project searches.

Short answer
- Use rlm_context_query for retrieving optimized context for a single question/task (the default, fast path).
- Use rlm_decompose when the request is complex and must be split into multiple dependent sub‑tasks (FULL-mode planning / multi‑chunk implementations).

Why (decision rules)
- rlm_context_query
  - Purpose: semantic search + ranking + token budgeting → returns the optimized context you feed to your LLM.
  - Use when the user asks a single question, a code lookup, or a specific implementation task that can be handled in one session/chunk.
  - Typical: bug fixes, small features, "How does X work?", "Find usages of Y", LITE-mode tasks.
  - Params to consider: max_tokens (3–6K common), search_mode ("hybrid" for best results).
  - Workflow rule: per the docs, ALWAYS try rlm_context_query first for documentation/code queries; if it succeeds, answer from the returned context.

- rlm_decompose
  - Purpose: break a complex problem into sub‑queries/sub‑tasks and identify dependencies and execution order.
  - Use when the work is multi-step, spans many files/modules, requires architecture/decision-making, or will be done across multiple sessions (FULL mode).
  - Typical: multi-module features, OAuth integration, multi-phase migrations, or anything you want chunked for iterative implementation/testing.
  - Common pattern: rlm_plan → rlm_decompose (max_depth controls granularity) → for each chunk call rlm_context_query to get chunk-specific context.

How they work together (recommended sequence)
1. Quick/single question:
   - rlm_context_query(query="…") → implement / answer.
2. Complex feature:
   - rlm_plan(query="Implement X") → get execution plan
   - rlm_decompose(query="Implement X", max_depth=1–2) → get chunks + dependencies
   - For each chunk:
     - context = rlm_context_query(query=chunk["query"], max_tokens=~6000, search_mode="hybrid")
     - implement / test / remember
3. Batch or parallel sub-queries: use rlm_multi_query or rlm_multi_project_query alongside decomposition when you need parallelism or cross-project search.

Examples
- “How does authentication work?” → rlm_context_query("authentication") (first try)
- “Implement OAuth 2.0 integration across API + frontend + DB” → rlm_plan → rlm_decompose → per-chunk rlm_context_query
- “Split large feature doc into implementable tasks” → rlm_decompose(max_depth=2) to create chunks

Practical heuristics
- If score ≥ 2 on the FULL-mode checklist (multi-session, 5+ files, architecture, future readers) → go FULL: plan + decompose + chunked rlm_context_query.
- If you want immediate context for coding or answering a small question → rlm_context_query.

Short answer: use hybrid for most queries. Pick keyword when you need exact, deterministic matches (identifiers, file paths, config), and semantic when you need conceptual/fuzzy matches (how‑it‑works, design intent, examples). Use rlm_search for regex/pattern searches.

Guidance and examples:

- Hybrid (recommended default)
  - Use when you want both relevance and exact matches.
  - Typical for implementation tasks: “implement authentication”, “user registration API”, or when you don’t know exact identifiers.
  - How to call: rlm_context_query(..., search_mode="hybrid")
  - Pros: best recall + precision balance; good for most workflows.

- Semantic
  - Use when the wording may differ or you want conceptual results.
  - Good for: “How does OAuth token refresh work?”, “Why do we use Redis for rate limiting?”, onboarding or architecture questions, design rationale.
  - Pros: finds relevant sections even if terminology differs.
  - Cons: can return broader matches; slightly less deterministic.

- Keyword
  - Use when you need exact matches or deterministic, high-precision results.
  - Good for: file names, function/class identifiers, exact config keys, and short, precise queries like “NEXTAUTH_URL” or “prisma schema model User”.
  - Pros: very precise and fast.
  - Cons: misses semantically relevant results that use different wording.

- rlm_search (regex / pattern search)
  - Use when you need regex/grep-like searches across code or docs (e.g., find occurrences of a specific import pattern or TODO comments).
  - Example: rlm_search for "getServerSideProps" or a regex to match env var usage.

Quick decision checklist
- I need fuzzy/conceptual results → Semantic
- I need exact identifier/file/config → Keyword
- I want both relevance + exactness (most cases) → Hybrid (default)
- I need regex/pattern matching in code → rlm_search

Extras
- For quick, predictable small queries use rlm_ask (~2500 tokens). For full, budgeted context use rlm_context_query with search_mode set appropriately.
- For cross-project discovery use rlm_multi_project_query (also supports search_mode).
- If Snipara returns errors (500/timeout), fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob per the docs.

Short answer: use
- keyword for exact, syntactic, or path/file searches;
- semantic for high-level, conceptual, or paraphrased queries;
- hybrid when you want both precision and recall (the safe default for most doc/code questions).

Guidance and examples

- Keyword
  - When: you know exact identifiers, filenames, error messages, config keys, or code snippets.
  - Why: strict matching (fast, precise) — returns literal matches you can open/edit.
  - Use cases: "Find occurrences of AUTH_SECRET", "open docs/CONTRIBUTING.md", "grep for 'TODO:'", or exact API names.
  - How: rlm_context_query(search_mode="keyword") or rlm_search / Read/Grep for known paths.

- Semantic
  - When: you want concepts, intent, how things work, or phrasing may vary (paraphrases).
  - Why: finds relevant sections even if wording differs (higher recall for conceptual questions).
  - Use cases: "How does authentication work?", "Explain OAuth flow used in project", "Best practice for token refresh".
  - How: rlm_context_query(search_mode="semantic").

- Hybrid (recommended default for most queries)
  - When: you need both literal matches and concept-level results (most developer/documentation queries).
  - Why: balances precision and recall — catches exact strings and related conceptual sections.
  - Use cases: "Implement user registration endpoint", "Find how we implemented rate limiting", multi-file feature work.
  - How: rlm_context_query(search_mode="hybrid") — safe default for LITE/FULL workflows.

Other tips
- If you need regex or pattern matching (TODOs, specific code patterns), use rlm_search (regex).
- For quick, predictable small-context queries use rlm_ask (built-in budget).
- For cross-project discovery (searching implementations across repos) prefer semantic or hybrid via rlm_multi_project_query.
- If Snipara returns unexpected results, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob tools per the non-negotiable workflow.

Example mapping
- Exact bug fix in one file → keyword (or direct Read)
- Understand design / architecture → semantic
- Implement feature spanning many files → hybrid
- Find similar implementations across projects → semantic/hybrid via rlm_multi_project_query

Use the project default (project.searchMode) when unsure; otherwise pick hybrid as the general-purpose option.

Short answer
- Use keyword for exact, syntactic, or token-level matches (filenames, config keys, code snippets).

Guidance and examples

1) Keyword (when to use)
- Best for: precise lookups where exact text/pattern matters.
- Examples:
  - “Find the .env key NAME=FRONTEND_URL”
  - “Where is `prisma.schema` imported?”
  - “Find the exact function `createSession()` implementation”
- Use tools: rlm_search for regex/pattern searches; rlm_context_query(search_mode="keyword") when you want the Snipara MCP to prioritize exact matches.
- Pros: very precise, low false positives.
- Cons: misses paraphrases and conceptual matches.

2) Semantic (when to use)
- Best for: conceptual, exploratory, or fuzzy queries that need understanding of meaning.
- Examples:
  - “How does authentication work?”
  - “What are the team coding standards for API errors?”
  - “Explain how rate-limiting is implemented across projects”
- Use rlm_context_query(search_mode="semantic").
- Pros: finds related content even if wording differs.
- Cons: may return broader/less precise results; tune token budget if you need more context.

3) Hybrid (when to use — recommended default)
- Best for: most practical queries where you want both precision and recall.
- Examples:
  - “Implement OAuth 2.0 integration” (FULL-mode plan & implementation)
  - “Implement user registration API endpoint” (include team standards)
  - Complex bug investigations spanning code + docs
- Use rlm_context_query(search_mode="hybrid") — this is used in our feature workflows and balances exact matches with semantic relevance.
- Pros: combines strengths of both modes; safer default for real tasks.

Practical tips & defaults
- Default behavior:
  - LITE tasks (~3–5K budget): hybrid if unsure; keyword if you know exact files.
  - FULL tasks (~6–15K budget): hybrid (or semantic for architecture-level exploration).
- If your query must search across multiple projects, use rlm_multi_project_query with hybrid or semantic as appropriate.
- If you know the exact file path, skip MCP and Read(file_path="...") directly (local tools are faster and simpler).
- When you need regex/pattern matching in code, use rlm_search or local grep.
- When in doubt: start with hybrid. If results are noisy, retry with keyword for precision or semantic for broader recall.

Example calls
- Quick exact search:
  rlm_context_query(query="createSession", search_mode="keyword", max_tokens=2500)
- High-level design:
  rlm_context_query(query="How does authentication work?", search_mode="semantic", max_tokens=6000)
- Default/complex chunk work:
  rlm_context_query(query="Implement OAuth token exchange", search_mode="hybrid", max_tokens=8000)

Fallbacks and reliability
- Always try Snipara MCP first (per rules). If you get 500/timeout, fall back to local Read/Grep/Glob.
- Use rlm_search for pattern-driven code searches when you need strict token-level matches.

Short answer — available auth methods
- Project API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Scope: single project
  - Use case: server-to-server calls for one project, CI, backends
- Team API Key
  - Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
  - Scope: all projects in a team
  - Use case: multi-project tools, team automation
- OAuth (device flow / bearer tokens)
  - Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - Scope: OAuth scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write"), has access & refresh tokens
  - Use case: end-user authentication for CLI/desktop/web apps (device flow for headless devices)
- End‑user sign-in (NextAuth)
  - Providers: GitHub, Google, Email (web sign-in flows)
  - Use case: web UI authentication and session management
- DeviceCode (part of OAuth device flow)
  - Temporary code with polling (deviceCode / userCode, statuses PENDING/AUTHORIZED/EXPIRED/USED/DENIED)
  - Use case: authenticating devices/CLI without embedded browser

How they differ in security (key differences and tradeoffs)
- Scope & principle of least privilege
  - Team API Key: highest blast radius — grants access across all team projects. Use only where team-wide access is required.
  - Project API Key: limited to a single project — preferred when you can scope access.
  - OAuth tokens: can be limited by scopes (fine-grained). Best for delegated user-level access.
- Lifespan & revocation
  - API Keys: typically long‑lived until revoked/rotated (unless you issue expiring keys). If leaked, immediate risk until revoked.
  - OAuth tokens: access tokens expire; refresh tokens can be revoked/rotated. Safer for ephemeral access.
- Auditability & identity
  - OAuth / NextAuth tie actions to user identity and scopes — better for per-user auditing and least-privilege delegation.
  - API keys are credential-based: actions are tied to the key, not a user (unless meta attached).
- Storage & handling
  - Server stores API keys and tokens hashed (docs: keys and tokens are hashed; only prefix stored). This reduces risk of DB leakage exposing full secrets.
  - Regardless, keys/tokens are bearer credentials; treat them as secrets: store in env, not in client JS, rotate regularly.
- Attack surface
  - Team API key compromise = broader impact than project key compromise.
  - Long-lived API keys are higher risk than short-lived OAuth access tokens.
  - Device flow increases risk if device codes are not polled/expired correctly — follow polling intervals and expiry.
- Transport & common pitfalls
  - Always use HTTPS. The docs specifically warn: use www.snipara.com for API POST endpoints to avoid 301 redirects that can change POST→GET (which can break flows).
- Server-side protections
  - Use per-key expiration, per-key rate limits, and monitoring. Revoke compromised keys immediately.
  - Prefer OAuth for end-user clients; prefer project-scoped API keys for server-to-server.

How credentials are represented in the DB (security notes from schema)
- ApiKey / TeamApiKey: stored hashed (keyHash), only keyPrefix stored for display.
- OAuthToken: access and refresh tokens stored as hashes; accessExpiresAt and refreshExpiresAt tracked.
- DeviceCode: has status and polling interval (default 5s).

Recommendations / best practices
- Use least privilege: prefer project keys over team keys; use OAuth scopes for user-level access.
- Prefer short-lived tokens where possible (OAuth access tokens + refresh).
- Rotate and revoke keys regularly; generate new keys if "Invalid API key".
- Store secrets server-side (env vars, secrets manager) and hash them in DB.
- Monitor usage (rlm usage tracking) and set alerts for anomalous queries.
- For CLI/desktop, use OAuth device flow (follow device code intervals) rather than embedding long-lived API keys.
- Avoid including bare snipara.com in POSTs (use www.snipara.com) to prevent POST→GET redirects.

Troubleshooting quick notes from docs
- “Invalid API key” → generate a new key in dashboard.
- “OAuth expired” → re-authenticate (refresh or full device flow).
- Test endpoints: health, tools/list, tools/call to validate auth and server behavior.

Summary — available auth options and how their security compares

1) Project API Key
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Scope: Single project
- Storage: server stores hashed key (keyHash) + keyPrefix for display; DB fields: expiresAt, revokedAt
- Use case: Server-to-server calls for a single project (MCP access)
- Security profile:
  - Medium: static secret — easy to use but higher risk if leaked.
  - Can be time-limited (expiresAt) and revoked (revokedAt) to limit blast radius.
  - Best practice: rotate regularly, store in vault, do not embed in client-side code.

2) Team API Key
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Scope: All projects in a team
- Storage: same hashed handling as project keys
- Use case: Tools or CI that need cross-project access for a team
- Security profile:
  - Lower security (higher blast radius) than project keys because it grants access to all team projects.
  - Use only when needed; apply stricter rotation and audit; limit distribution to trusted automation.

3) OAuth (Device Flow)
- Header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Flow: Device code is issued (deviceCode, userCode). Client polls until user authorizes. Access + refresh tokens stored hashed in DB (accessTokenHash, refreshTokenHash). DeviceCode status: PENDING/AUTHORIZED/EXPIRED/USED/DENIED.
- Use case: CLI, desktop apps, other interactive clients where redirect-based OAuth is impractical
- Security profile:
  - High when implemented correctly: tokens are short-lived, can be scoped, and refresh tokens can be rotated/revoked.
  - Safer than static API keys for user-level auth because user consent and scopes apply.
  - Watch out for implementation pitfalls: POST endpoints must use the exact host (use www.snipara.com for POSTs — otherwise a 301 redirect may convert POST→GET and break flow).

4) NextAuth social/email sign-ins (GitHub, Google, Email)
- Use case: Web sign-in for users via NextAuth providers
- Security profile:
  - High: relies on OAuth providers and NextAuth best-practices (session cookies, provider MFA where available).
  - Good for end-user authentication; tie into team/project membership and roles.

5) Enterprise SSO (SAML / OIDC / Auth0)
- Config flag: team.ssoEnabled, team.ssoProvider
- Use case: Enterprise customers requiring SSO for team access
- Security profile:
  - Very high: centralized identity, enforced policies (MFA, SCIM, conditional access), reduced credential sprawl.
  - Recommended for orgs with many users and strict compliance needs.

6) Short-lived access tokens / refresh tokens
- Stored hashed; accessExpiresAt / refreshExpiresAt tracked
- Security profile:
  - High when used (short lifetime + refresh rotation). Prefer over long-lived static tokens.

Other implementation/security details from the docs
- Keys and tokens are stored hashed (only prefixes shown), reducing risk if DB is compromised.
- Device flow: polling interval is provided; statuses allow safe lifetime handling.
- Preflight checks: “Invalid API key” → regenerate; OAuth expired → re-run device flow.
- Admin controls: expire and revoke keys (expiresAt, revokedAt) to limit exposure.
- Team vs Project: choose lowest-privilege key needed — team keys have broader access and therefore higher risk.

Practical recommendations / best practices
- Principle of least privilege: use project-scoped keys when possible; use team keys only if cross-project access is required.
- Prefer OAuth device flow (user tokens) for CLI/desktop clients and user-scoped operations.
- For server-to-server automation, prefer short-lived tokens or rotate API keys frequently and store them in a secret manager.
- Always use HTTPS and pass the correct host for POST endpoints (use www.snipara.com to avoid POST→GET redirects).
- Enforce expirations, revoke compromised keys immediately, and audit usage (Query usage logs exist).
- Use Enterprise SSO for large orgs to centralize identity and improve security posture.

1) Project API Key
- Header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Scope: single project

- Scope: tokens include scopes (e.g., "mcp:read mcp:write")
- Security tradeoffs: standard OAuth gives short‑lived access tokens, refresh tokens, scoping, and revocation → safer for user-level access and third‑party clients. Recommended for end-user integrations and clients on untrusted devices.

- Security tradeoffs: strongest option for enterprises — centralized identity, enforced policies, MFA, account provisioning/deprovisioning, audit. Use for team-wide admin control.

6) DeviceCode model (internal to device flow)
- Purpose: temporary polling codes (deviceCode/userCode) with status (PENDING/AUTHORIZED/EXPIRED/USED/DENIED)
- Security tradeoffs: allows secure device authentication without embedding user credentials.

- ApiKey and OAuth tokens are hashed in storage (reduces theft risk on server breach).
- ApiKey and TeamApiKey include expiresAt and revokedAt fields — use expirations and revocation for risk mitigation.
- OAuth tokens support scopes and expiry — prefer OAuth for least privilege and user-scoped access.

Recommendations (practical)
- Prefer OAuth/device flow for user-level integrations and third‑party apps (scoped, revocable, short‑lived).

Short answer
- By default Snipara splits the query token budget between the project docs and shared context: 60% project / 40% shared.
- The shared-context portion is then allocated across categories using fixed percentages: MANDATORY 40% / BEST_PRACTICES 30% / GUIDELINES 20% / REFERENCE 10%.
- Within each category, documents are ranked (by priority and relevance) and sections are included until that category's token budget is exhausted.

Details / how to control it
- When you call rlm_context_query, Snipara automatically includes shared context and applies the 60/40 split to your max_tokens (or the project default).
- You can call rlm_shared_context(max_tokens=...) directly to see the exact documents returned and the budget_allocation object that shows per-category token allocations.
- Document priority (0–100) and precomputed tokenCount are used to weight/choose documents inside a category.

Example
- If max_tokens = 6000:
  - Project docs get 60% → 3,600 tokens
  - Shared context gets 40% → 2,400 tokens
    - MANDATORY: 40% of 2,400 = 960 tokens
    - BEST_PRACTICES: 30% = 720 tokens
    - GUIDELINES: 20% = 480 tokens
    - REFERENCE: 10% = 240 tokens

Notes / risks
- Priorities on shared documents (and their tokenCount) affect what actually fits into each category budget.
- You can observe the exact allocation returned by rlm_shared_context (it returns budget_allocation and selected documents). See docs/SHARED_CONTEXT.md for full behavior and examples.

Short answer

- The shared-context portion is then allocated across the four document categories by priority order: MANDATORY (40%), BEST_PRACTICES (30%), GUIDELINES (20%), REFERENCE (10%).
- Within each category, individual shared documents are ranked by their priority field (0–100) and included until the category’s token allotment is exhausted.

How to inspect/control it
- Use rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens=...) — Snipara will automatically include shared context and apply the split (default 60/40) against the max_tokens you pass.
- Or call rlm_shared_context(categories=[...], max_tokens=...) to explicitly request shared context and see the returned budget_allocation object (shows per-category token allotments and the documents selected).
- You can filter which categories to include (e.g., MANDATORY + BEST_PRACTICES) and set max_tokens to control the overall budget.

Example (from docs)
- rlm_shared_context(max_tokens=4000) returned:
  - budget_allocation: MANDATORY=40% (≈1139), BEST_PRACTICES=30% (≈854), GUIDELINES=20% (≈569), REFERENCE=10% (≈285)
  - documents selected per category are chosen in priority order until the category budget is used.

Notes / implications
- Shared context counts against your total token budget for the query (it’s not “free”).
- Document-level priority (0–100) and collection/document priorities influence which documents are included when budgets are tight.
- If you need a different split or larger shared-context window, increase max_tokens or explicitly fetch shared context first (rlm_shared_context) and inject it into the session.

Short answer
- There is no documented built‑in “Google Docs / Live edit” real‑time collaborative editor in Snipara. The docs do not describe synchronous co‑editing features (cursor presence, OT/CRDT, live conflict resolution).
- To collaborate in (near) real time you should use the supported team workflows: GitHub‑backed editing + Snipara indexing, Shared Context Collections for shared rules/templates, and the VS Code extension / Claude plugin for tight integration. For true live editing you’ll need an external real‑time editor (e.g., VS Code Live Share, Google Docs) and then sync into Snipara.

Recommended approaches and how to configure them

1) Git-backed collaboration (recommended for source-controlled docs)
- Use the project’s GitHub repo as the source of truth and enable repo sync:
  - Ensure project.githubSyncEnabled = true for the project (see Project model).
  - Push edits via branches/PRs so teammates can collaborate with full history and code review.
  - Snipara will index/sync documents (docs are indexed as Document / DocumentChunk) so searches and rlm_context_query will see updates after sync.
- Benefit: full audit trail, PR review, CI, and safe patch-only work.

2) Shared Context Collections (team standards, templates, docs you want auto-included)
- Create a Shared Context Collection and upload team documents (MANDATORY / BEST_PRACTICES / GUIDELINES).
  - Example CLI/API: rlm_upload_shared_document(collection_id="team_collection_id", title="TypeScript Coding Standards", content="...", category="BEST_PRACTICES", priority=90)
- Link the collection to your projects in the dashboard so all project queries automatically include team standards.
- Developers get the same enforced context; good for consistent reviews and automated checks.
- See docs/SHARED_CONTEXT.md for details.

3) VS Code extension + local collaboration workflow
- Install Snipara VS Code extension (ext install snipara.snipara) for in‑editor integration (search, inject context, RLM runtime bridge).
- For synchronous editing inside VS Code, pair the Snipara extension with an external live‑edit tool (VS Code Live Share). Live Share handles cursors/real‑time editing; commit/push when ready so Snipara can index changes.
- Note: the docs state hooks (PreCompact, SessionStart, PostToolUse) are CLI concepts and are not available in the VS Code extension — the extension uses activate/deactivate lifecycle events (see docs/VSCODE_EXTENSION.md).

4) Quick updates / ad hoc docs: rlm_upload_document & rlm_inject
- For quick, immediate additions you can upload documents directly:
  - rlm_upload_document(path="docs/feature.md", content="<content>")
  - Or inject session context temporarily: rlm_inject(context="...", append=False)
- Use when you need immediate indexing without a Git PR cycle.

What to do if you need true built-in real‑time collaboration
- The platform as documented doesn't implement OT/CRDT or concurrent editing primitives. If you require a native real‑time editor inside Snipara:
  - Option A: Use external live editors + GitHub/rlm_upload_document sync (practical, lowest risk).
  - Option B: Request/track a product feature to build real‑time co‑editing (would require design: editor choice, CRDT/OT layer, locking, auth/permissions, indexing semantics, security). I can draft a feature spec / implementation plan if you want.

Useful docs & commands
- Shared context: docs/SHARED_CONTEXT.md
- VS Code extension: docs/VSCODE_EXTENSION.md
- Upload document: rlm_upload_document(...)
- Inject session context: rlm_inject(...)
- Enable project repo sync: set project.githubSyncEnabled = true (and link repo in dashboard)
- For templates: rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template

Short answer
- Each Context plan has a monthly query quota (FREE 100/mo, PRO 5,000/mo, TEAM 20,000/mo, ENTERPRISE = unlimited).
- When you exceed your plan quota, additional context-optimization queries are blocked until the quota resets (start of next billing month) or you move to a higher plan / resolve billing — the platform enforces the plan limit and further queries will fail.

Details & how to handle it
- Verify usage: use the dashboard or the MCP tool (rlm_stats) to view current usage. Example MCP call (from the docs):
  curl -s -X POST "https://api.snipara.com/mcp/snipara" \
    -H "X-API-Key: <your-key>" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
    -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"rlm_stats","arguments":{}}}'
- Check subscription state: billing/subscription statuses (ACTIVE, PAST_DUE, UNPAID, etc.) can also affect access — unresolved billing may suspend queries.
- Remediation:
  1. Wait for the monthly quota to reset.
  2. Upgrade your Context plan (via the dashboard / billing) to increase monthly quota.
  3. Contact support / billing if you believe the limit is incorrect.

Notes / edge-cases
- ENTERPRISE plans are unlimited.
- All usage is tracked (Query model: input/output tokens, success flag, latency, projectId), so blocked/failed calls will be recorded for auditing.

What to do next (recommended order)

Short answer
- Snipara counts real tokens (not characters) with tiktoken (cl100k_base) and then greedily selects the most relevant document sections until the requested token budget is used. If a section only partially fits, it smart-truncates at a sentence boundary and marks it truncated.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
1. Token counting
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to encode text and measure token counts exactly.
   - All selection decisions are based on token counts from that encoder.

2. Query & search modes
   - The rlm_context_query tool supports search_mode = "keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid".
   - Candidate sections are gathered by the chosen search(s) and scored for relevance.

3. Ranking and greedy selection
   - Candidates are ranked by relevance score.
   - A greedy algorithm picks the highest‑relevance sections first, adding their token_count to a running total.
   - Selection stops when adding the next whole section would exceed max_tokens.

4. Partial fits and smart_truncate()
   - If a section does not fully fit but there is remaining budget, smart_truncate() is used:
     - Truncates to fit remaining tokens,
     - Cuts at the nearest sentence boundary (. ! ?) to avoid chopping mid-sentence,
     - Appends "..." and marks the section as truncated in metadata.

5. Metadata & response
   - Each selected section includes: title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag (if applicable).
   - The response contains sections[], total_tokens, truncated (global), session_context (if available), and suggestions.
   - Session context and system instructions may be auto-injected; session context inclusion is limited (implementation notes: up to ~20% of budget).

6. Budget allocation rules (shared/project docs)
   - Default allocations control how token budgets are split across categories (project vs shared categories). Example defaults:
     - project: 0.40 (40%)
     - MANDATORY: 0.25, BEST_PRACTICES: 0.20, GUIDELINES: 0.10, REFERENCE: 0.05
   - Documents are allocated within their category budgets and sorted by relevance; docs that exceed category budgets are marked unallocated.
   - Category priority is used where applicable to process higher priority categories first.

7. Limits, metrics and tooling
   - Hard limits exist (e.g., max tokens per collection).
   - Tools like multi-query and decompose use the same token-aware logic to split/estimate budgets per subquery.
   - Token efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct) and cost tracking are supported to estimate savings and monetary cost.

Example request/response (conceptual)
- Request: rlm_context_query(query="How does authentication work?", max_tokens=4000, search_mode="hybrid")
- Engine: search → rank → count tokens with tiktoken → pick sections until 4000 tokens used → partially truncate last section if needed.
- Response: JSON with sections (file + lines + token_count + relevance_score + truncated flags), total_tokens, truncated boolean, session_context, suggestions.

Why this design
- Accurate token accounting (tiktoken) ensures what is sent to the LLM fits model limits.
- Greedy relevance-first selection yields the most useful context for a given budget.
- Smart truncation preserves coherence by cutting at sentence boundaries.
- Category/project allocations and hard limits let Snipara prioritize mission‑critical content while enforcing safety caps.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a strict token budget when building context for a query: it counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranks candidate sections by relevance, and greedily includes the highest‑relevance sections until the token budget is exhausted. If a section only partially fits, it smart‑truncates at a sentence boundary and marks it truncated.

How it works (details)
- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to count tokens precisely (same encoding family as GPT‑4/Claude).
  - Every candidate section has a computed token_count and those counts drive selection and limits.

- Budget parameters
  - The context tool accepts max_tokens (default 4000) as the token budget for returned context.
  - Session context can be included (configurable) and is limited (implementation note: up to ~20% of the budget).
  - System instructions are auto-injected into the context as required (counted toward budget).

- Candidate selection algorithm
  - Search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid (combines both).
  - Sections are ranked by relevance score.
  - Greedy selection: include highest relevance sections first until adding the next would exceed the budget.
  - If a section partially fits, smart_truncate() is used to fit remaining tokens.

- Smart truncation
  - Truncates to remaining token allowance without cutting mid‑sentence.
  - Looks for sentence boundaries (., !, ?) and cuts there, appending "..." and setting truncated: true.
  - Ensures coherent end-of-section text when partial content is returned.

- Metadata and output
  - The rlm_context_query response returns detailed metadata per section:
    - title, content, file path, lines (start/end), relevance_score, token_count, truncated flag when applicable.
  - Response also includes total_tokens used, truncated (boolean if the overall result hit the budget), session_context (if any), and suggestions (e.g., related docs).
  - Usage/diagnostics include search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit, etc.

- Budget allocation / category logic
  - Shared documents can be budgeted by category with default allocations (examples):
    - MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% (category budgets in docs).
  - Project docs typically get priority (configurable allocation like project: 40%).
  - Token allocation functions divide the total budget by category and allocate within each category based on relevance until that category’s tokens run out.

- Multi-query & decomposition support
  - There are recursive/parallel tools:
    - Decompose: break a complex query into sub-queries, estimate tokens, and produce an execution plan (no LLM calls).
    - Multi-query: runs multiple context queries in parallel with per-query budgets and aggregates token usage.

- Limits & metrics
  - Hard limits enforced (examples: MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION).
  - Token efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) measure savings from optimized context without calling an LLM.
  - Cost tracking can be tied to token_budget and cost_budget_usd for higher‑level completions.

Short answer
- Snipara treats tokens as the primary currency for building LLM inputs and automatically selects/document snippets to include so the combined context fits a configured token budget (e.g., 4000 tokens).
- It counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), scores candidate sections for relevance, greedily adds the highest‑relevance sections until the budget is exhausted, and if a section only partially fits it smart‑truncates at sentence boundaries and marks it as truncated.

Key concepts (how it works end‑to‑end)
1. Token counting
   - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to measure token counts for any candidate content and for budgeting decisions (more accurate than characters/words).
   - Every candidate section has an associated token_count computed via the encoder.

2. Candidate retrieval & scoring
   - Search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid (fuse keyword + semantic results).
   - Candidate sections are ranked by relevance (relevance_score).

3. Greedy selection within the token budget
   - Given max_tokens (default 4000), Snipara iterates ranked candidates from highest relevance down.
   - If an entire section fits, it is added.
   - If adding the whole section would exceed the budget but some tokens remain, the engine uses smart_truncate to include a partial section that fits exactly.
   - Selection stops when the budget is used up.

4. Smart truncation
   - Truncates by token budget but snaps to natural sentence boundaries (., !, ?), not mid-sentence.
   - Appends an ellipsis marker ("...") and marks section metadata truncated: true.
   - Ensures the included text is coherent and respects token limits.

5. Metadata and response
   - Returned sections include title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count and truncated flag when applicable.
   - The tool returns total_tokens used, truncated flag for the overall response, session_context (if present), and suggestions (related docs).
   - System instructions and recent session context can be auto‑injected (session context limited to a portion of the budget, e.g., up to ~20%).

6. Budget allocation and priorities (shared/project docs)
   - Token budgets can be allocated by category (examples: MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) or by source (project vs shared).
   - Default allocation weights guide how much of the total budget is reserved for each category.
   - Within each allocation, documents are selected by relevance until that category’s share is used.
   - Hard limits are enforced (e.g., max tokens per collection).

7. Multi‑query and recursive tooling
   - There are higher‑level tools that decompose queries into subqueries and/or run multiple context queries in parallel with per‑query budgets.
   - Decomposers estimate tokens per subquery and produce an execution plan without calling an LLM.

8. Instrumentation & metrics
   - Responses include search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache hits, and token usage metrics.
   - Token efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct) help measure how much context/token cost was saved by optimization.

Primary tool & parameters
- Tool name: rlm_context_query
- Key parameters: query (string), max_tokens (int, e.g., 4000), search_mode ("keyword" | "semantic" | "hybrid"), include_metadata (bool), optional session_id
- Result: a structured payload with selected sections, token usage, truncated flags, session context, and suggestions.

Why this matters
- Accurate token counting + prioritized selection maximizes useful context given tight model budgets.
- Smart truncation keeps included text readable and avoids breaking sentences.
- Category/project allocations ensure mission‑critical docs get priority while still allowing flexible budgets for extras.
- Combined, these features reduce LLM costs and improve answer relevance by sending only the most pertinent content to the model.

Shared Context Collections are team- or org-level libraries of reusable knowledge — coding standards, security rules, architecture decisions, best practices, templates and reference material — that are automatically injected into project queries so every developer and automated workflow follows the same rules.

How they work (overview)
- Create a collection (team admin): upload documents that capture rules, best practices, guidelines, examples and templates.
- Link collections to projects (dashboard): projects that link the collection automatically inherit its documents.
- Query time: when you call rlm_shared_context or rlm_context_query with include_shared_context=true, the system merges shared context with project docs and returns the combined context. Shared context is loaded first and can be auto-injected into sessions so every subsequent query sees the team standards.
- Enforcement: MANDATORY documents are treated as non‑negotiable rules and are included by default. Best practices and guidelines are included according to priority and token budget allocation so the assistant can recommend and enforce team conventions.

Key concepts
- Categories & token budget (default distribution)
  - MANDATORY — highest priority (40% token budget) — non‑negotiables (security, architecture)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — high (30%) — recommended patterns and conventions
  - GUIDELINES — medium (20%) — helpful suggestions
  - REFERENCE — low (10%) — background info and examples
- Priorities: documents carry a 0–100 priority score to rank importance within a category.
- Injection & ordering: shared context is queried/merged first (a configurable percent of the budget), then project docs are queried with the remaining budget. Returned sections place shared context before local project sections.

Common tools / API calls
- rlm_list_collections — list collections available to your team (public + private)
- rlm_upload_shared_document — upload a new document into a collection
- rlm_shared_context — fetch merged shared context (filter by categories, set max_tokens)
- rlm_context_query — project query that automatically includes shared context (token budgets split)
- rlm_inject — inject shared context into a session so it applies to all subsequent queries
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — access prompt templates stored in collections
- rlm_multi_project_query — search across all team projects (uses shared context too)

Practical workflow (simple)
1. Admin creates a collection and uploads MANDATORY rules, BEST_PRACTICES, etc.
2. Admin links the collection to team projects.
3. Developers call rlm_shared_context at session start or use rlm_inject to add standards to session context.
4. Every implementation, code review, and query will include and obey those standards automatically.
5. Update the collection over time; changes propagate to linked projects.

Plan / access notes
- Team or Enterprise plan required for team-wide shared collections and auto-enforcement features (not available on Free/Pro).
- Enterprise adds features like custom categories and analytics.

Best practices
- Put true non‑negotiables in MANDATORY docs.
- Keep each document focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priority to resolve conflicts within a category.
- Provide templates for consistent code reviews and PR checks.
- Update collections as standards evolve.

- Show example calls (rlm_upload_shared_document and rlm_shared_context) tailored to your stack.

Short answer — Snipara uses the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628). Flow and implementation details:

1. CLI starts the device flow
   - Client calls POST /api/oauth/device/code with { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
   - Example response:
     - device_code (opaque string the client will poll with)
     - user_code (human-readable code, e.g. "ABCD-1234")
     - verification_uri (e.g. https://snipara.com/device)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL with code pre-filled)
     - expires_in (e.g. 900 seconds)
     - interval (poll interval, e.g. 5s)

2. User authorizes in the browser
   - CLI prints/opens the verification URL and user_code (or directs user to verification_uri_complete).
   - User visits the URL, signs in, and enters/approves the user_code.

3. Client polls for the token
   - The CLI polls the token endpoint (per RFC 8628) using the device_code until the user authorizes or the device_code expires.
   - On success the server returns an access token.

4. Tokens are stored locally
   - Snipara stores OAuth tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json for future requests.

5. How tokens are used by the client/server
   - When present, OAuth tokens are preferred. Authorization header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - If no valid OAuth token is found, the client falls back to API key auth: X-API-Key: rlm_...
   - You can force API-key-only behavior by deleting or backing up ~/.snipara/tokens.json or by setting SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment.

Troubleshooting / notes
- Typical device_code lifetime in examples is short (expires_in 900s). Poll at the provided interval.
- If OAuth expires or is revoked, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.
- To bypass OAuth entirely (e.g., for automated runs), set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true or remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json so the client uses the API key instead.

Short answer — Snipara uses the standard OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628). In practice:

How it works (step‑by‑step)
- Client (CLI) calls the device-code endpoint with its client_id:
  - POST /api/oauth/device/code  { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
- Server responds with device_code, user_code, verification_uri (and verification_uri_complete), expires_in, and a polling interval. Example:
  - { "device_code":"abc123...", "user_code":"ABCD-1234", "verification_uri":"https://snipara.com/device", "verification_uri_complete":"https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234", "expires_in":900, "interval":5 }
- CLI shows the user_code and verification URL (or the verification_uri_complete link) and instructs the user to open it in a browser and complete sign-in/consent.
- Meanwhile the CLI polls the token endpoint (at the interval returned) exchanging the device_code for an access token until the user authorizes or the device_code expires.
- On success the server returns an access token (used as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...), which the client stores (by default in ~/.snipara/tokens.json) for future calls.

How Snipara clients use the token
- When making MCP requests the client prefers an OAuth token (Authorization: Bearer ...) found in ~/.snipara/tokens.json.
- If no valid OAuth token is available it falls back to API key auth (X-API-Key: rlm_...).

Troubleshooting & alternatives
- If OAuth expires or fails, re-run the device flow to re-authenticate.
- To force API-key-only authentication instead of OAuth:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the client environment to skip ~/.snipara/tokens.json and use SNIPARA_API_KEY.
  - Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv) so the client falls back to the API key.
- Header formats:
  - OAuth: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
  - API key: X-API-Key: rlm_...

That’s the full flow — device-code issuance → user completes auth in browser → CLI polls for token → token stored and used for subsequent requests.

- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization (device) flow (RFC 8628). A CLI (or other limited-input client) asks the server for a device/user code, instructs the user to complete auth in a browser, then polls the server for the issued token. Tokens are stored locally and used as a Bearer Authorization header once issued.

Step-by-step (how it works in Snipara)
1. Client requests a device code:
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code with body { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
   - Server returns device_code, user_code, verification_uri, verification_uri_complete, expires_in, interval.
   - Example response (from docs):
     {
       "device_code": "abc123...",
       "user_code": "ABCD-1234",
       "verification_uri": "https://snipara.com/device",
       "verification_uri_complete": "https://snipara.com/device?code=ABCD-1234",
       "expires_in": 900,
       "interval": 5
     }

2. Show the user what to do:
   - CLI shows the verification URI and user_code (or the verification_uri_complete link).
   - User opens the URL and enters/approves the code in the browser and completes sign-in/consent.

3. Client polls for tokens:
   - The client polls the token endpoint at the server-specified interval (e.g., every 5s) until the user completes auth or the device_code expires (expires_in seconds).
   - When successful the server returns an access token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...) and possibly refresh token.

4. Local storage and usage:
   - The client saves OAuth tokens (e.g., in ~/.snipara/tokens.json).
   - When present, the client prefers OAuth tokens for MCP requests (Authorization: Bearer ...). If no OAuth token is available, it falls back to API key auth (X-API-Key: rlm_...).

Important Snipara-specific notes & troubleshooting
- Priority: client checks ~/.snipara/tokens.json first, then falls back to API key environment variables.
- To force using an API key instead of OAuth, either:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env (recommended), or
  - Remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv).

Snipara uses a strict layered (tiered) architecture:

- Routes (app/api/) — thin HTTP handlers that authenticate, validate (Zod), and delegate to services.
- Services (lib/services/) — external API adapters and business logic (lazy init + retry for external clients).
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — centralized CRUD + permission checks; the only place to access Prisma.
- Utils & Types (types/, lib/utils/) — pure helpers and types with NO dependencies.

Rules: lower layers cannot import from higher ones; route handlers must remain thin (requireAuth → schema.parse → call service → return response).

Snipara uses a strict layered (n‑tier) architecture with clear separation of concerns:

- Layer order / import hierarchy (lower layers cannot import from higher):
  Routes (app/api/) → Services (lib/services/) → Repositories (lib/db/queries/) → Utils & Types (no dependencies)

- Responsibilities:
  - Routes: thin HTTP handlers — authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to services/queries, return responses.
  - Services: external API adapters (lazy init, retries), business orchestration.
  - Repositories: centralized CRUD only (Prisma access), include permission checks; never call Prisma from routes/services directly.
  - Utils & Types: pure helpers and shared types with no imports from other layers.

- Patterns/enforcements:
  - Route handler pattern (requireAuth → schema.parse → delegate → successResponse/handleError).
  - Repository pattern for DB access.
  - Services pattern for external integrations (lazy client init, retry).
  - Lower layers cannot import from higher layers; utils/types must be dependency‑free.

In short: a layered service + repository architecture with thin controllers (routes), service adapters, and a centralized repository layer.

Short answer
